title: Opioids — Conversion & Equianalgesia
oneLiner: Safe switching between opioids using standardised ratios to avoid under-treatment or overdose; requires careful dose calculation and monitoring.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Respiratory depression / opioid overdose:**  
  - **Single finding:** respiratory rate <10/min or SpO₂ <92% on room air, with pinpoint pupils and drowsiness.  
  - Action: *Stop all opioids, give naloxone 200-400mcg IV/IM, repeat as needed, call 2222 if unresponsive.*  
- **Opioid-induced neurotoxicity (e.g. myoclonus, hyperalgesia, seizures):**  
  - **Single finding:** new generalised myoclonus or worsening pain on escalating doses (hallmark of morphine-3-glucuronide accumulation).  
  - Action: *Switch to a different opioid (e.g. fentanyl, oxycodone) and reduce dose by 50% – discuss with pain team.*
sections.Presentation:
- Patient being switched from one opioid to another (e.g. from morphine to oxycodone)  
- Pain not controlled despite dose escalation  
- New adverse effects (constipation, nausea, sedation) prompting rotation  
- Patient on one opioid pre-admission, now requires another (e.g. IV instead of oral)  
- Prior opioid exposure unknown – need to calculate baseline
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Inadequate analgesia from wrong conversion:** Pain score unchanged or worse after switch  
  - *Differentiator:* Review actual dose given vs calculated; check for missed breakthrough doses  
- **Opioid toxicity from over-conversion:** Sedation, confusion, reduced respiratory rate  
  - *Splitter:* Sedation score ≥2 (drowsy but rousable) + RR <12 → likely toxicity, not undertreatment  
- **Neuropathic pain not responding to opioids:** Pain is burning, shooting, or allodynic  
  - *Differentiator:* Add gabapentinoid or amitriptyline, not escalate opioid  
- **Opioid-induced hyperalgesia:** Paradoxical worsening of pain on opioid escalation  
  - *Differentiator:* Pain worsens with increasing dose, no new pathology; reduce opioid or rotate
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Pain score (0-10), sedation score (1-4), respiratory rate, SpO₂  
- **Bloods:** U&Es and creatinine (estimate eGFR for morphine, codeine); LFTs if using methadone or buprenorphine  
- **Imaging:** Not routine unless suspect new pathology (e.g. spinal cord compression)  
- **Special tests:** Opioid plasma levels **not** routinely available or useful  
- **Never-miss test:** **eGFR** before converting to morphine or codeine – active metabolites accumulate (morphine-6-glucuronide, codeine → morphine) → risk of toxicity in renal impairment
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ward start):**  
  - Confirm eGFR, baseline pain score, sedation level  
  - Calculate total daily dose of current opioid (all routes – regular + breakthrough)  
  - Use an **equianalgesic conversion table** (see Drugs & Doses)  
  - **Reduce the calculated dose by 25-50%** for complete cross-tolerance (especially when switching from high doses or between classes)  
  - Prescribe **immediate-release (IR) breakthrough** every 1-2 hours PRN at 10-20% of the regular 24-hour dose (e.g. for morphine oral 60mg/24h → PRN 5-10mg PO 1-2 hourly)  
  - Document new regular opioid + PRN in drug chart, sign and time  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Reassess pain at 4-6 hours post-switch; if pain >5, give breakthrough and consider increasing regular dose by 25-50% after 24h (do not escalate faster)  
  - Monitor sedation score, RR, SpO₂ every 4 hours for 24 hours after rotation  
  - If using **fentanyl patch**: apply to clean dry non-hairy skin; onset 12-24h; do not use for acute pain or unstable doses – allow 12h overlap with oral opioid, then stop previous opioid  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Once stable (pain controlled, stable dose for 48h), switch to long-acting formulation (modified-release)  
  - Arrange GP/pain clinic follow-up for continued monitoring  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: nil special unless NBM – then IV maintenance (e.g. 1L 0.9% NaCl + 1L 5% glucose/24h)  
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40mg SC OD) unless contraindicated  
  - Diet: normal, but encourage high-fibre + fluids to prevent constipation  
  - Observations: NEWS2, plus sedation score and pain score 4-hourly
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Equianalgesic conversions** (oral morphine equivalent – OME):  
  - Morphine PO : IV = 3 : 1 (e.g. 10mg PO ≈ 3.3mg IV)  
  - Codeine 60mg ≈ Morphine 6mg PO  
  - Oxycodone PO : Morphine PO = 1 : 1.5 (i.e. 10mg oxycodone ≈ 15mg morphine)  
  - Tramadol 100mg PO ≈ Morphine 10mg PO (weak evidence – use cautiously)  
  - Fentanyl patch conversion: **[check local policy]** – typically 12.5mcg/h patch ≈ oral morphine 30mg/24h (some sources say 25mcg/h ≈ 60mg/24h; **never assume without verifying**)  
  - Buprenorphine patch conversion: **[check local policy]** – low strength (5, 10, 20mcg/h) for moderate pain; do **not** use for high opioid requirement  
  - **Breakthrough dose:** IR morphine/oxycodone 10-20% of 24-hour OME per dose  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - IV morphine (for NBM/acute) – usual starting dose 2.5-5mg IV, titrate  
  - Oxycodone PO or IV (no active metabolite, better in renal impairment) – IV dose 1/2 of PO dose  
  - Fentanyl IV (short-acting, for procedures or renal failure)  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - Avoid codeine/tramadol in CYP2D6 poor metabolisers (no effect) or ultra-rapid metabolisers (toxicity) – check local guidance  
  - Avoid morphine in acute renal impairment (eGFR <30) – use fentanyl, oxycodone or alfentanil  
  - Avoid buprenorphine in severe respiratory compromise or opioid dependence (precipitates withdrawal)  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - **Renal impairment (eGFR <30):** avoid morphine, codeine, dihydrocodeine; reduce oxycodone by 50% or use fentanyl/alfentanil  
  - **Hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh B/C):** reduce morphine/oxycodone/fentanyl dose by 50%; avoid tramadol  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Not reducing dose when rotating to a different opioid** – assuming 1:1 conversion without accounting for incomplete cross-tolerance → respiratory arrest. **Always reduce by 25-50%.**  
  - **Fentanyl patch – cutting it or applying to compromised skin** – rapid release → overdose. **Never cut.**  
  - **Using a fentanyl patch for breakthrough pain** – onset is too slow.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** respiratory depression, sedation, falls (especially in elderly), severe constipation (opioid-induced bowel dysfunction)  
- **Chronic:** tolerance, dependence, opioid-induced hyperalgesia, hypogonadism, immunosuppression (long-term)
sections.Prognosis:
- With careful conversion and titration, 70-80% of patients will achieve adequate analgesia; failure often requires specialist pain team input  
- Opioid rotation success rate ~60%; when it fails, consider multimodal analgesia and referral
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- Always **calculate the total daily dose** (regular + breakthrough) before any switch – include all PRN doses given in last 24h  
- Document the **conversion method** in the notes (e.g. “morphine 60mg/24h PO → oxycodone 40mg/24h PO, with 50% reduction for cross-tolerance”)  
- Provide a written **pain plan** on discharge: regular dose + PRN regimen, and who to contact if not working
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- Do NOT use **codeine or tramadol** as breakthrough for a patient on high-dose morphine – they are weak and may not work  
- Do NOT switch directly from oral morphine to a fentanyl patch without calculating dose – **always use an equianalgesic table and check local policy**  
- Do NOT forget to **stop the previous opioid** when initiating transdermal patch – list both as regular once only for first 24h, then discontinue old opioid
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** pain not controlled after 2-3 breakthrough doses, increased drowsiness or confusion, or new difficulty breathing  
- **Follow-up:** GP within 48h for dose review; pain clinic referral arranged if high-risk or rotation needed
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / pain nurse:** pain score remains ≥7 despite two breakthrough doses within 4 hours, or if rotation calculation is complex (e.g. methadone, multiple opioids)  
- **Call crash team (2222):** respiratory rate <8/min, GCS <9, SpO₂ <90% on air, or severe opioid-induced neurotoxicity (seizures, myoclonus)

rawMarkdown:
## Opioids — Conversion & Equianalgesia
**One-liner:** Safe switching between opioids using standardised ratios to avoid under-treatment or overdose; requires careful dose calculation and monitoring.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Respiratory depression / opioid overdose:**  
  - **Single finding:** respiratory rate <10/min or SpO₂ <92% on room air, with pinpoint pupils and drowsiness.  
  - Action: *Stop all opioids, give naloxone 200-400mcg IV/IM, repeat as needed, call 2222 if unresponsive.*  
- **Opioid-induced neurotoxicity (e.g. myoclonus, hyperalgesia, seizures):**  
  - **Single finding:** new generalised myoclonus or worsening pain on escalating doses (hallmark of morphine-3-glucuronide accumulation).  
  - Action: *Switch to a different opioid (e.g. fentanyl, oxycodone) and reduce dose by 50% – discuss with pain team.*

### Presentation
- Patient being switched from one opioid to another (e.g. from morphine to oxycodone)  
- Pain not controlled despite dose escalation  
- New adverse effects (constipation, nausea, sedation) prompting rotation  
- Patient on one opioid pre-admission, now requires another (e.g. IV instead of oral)  
- Prior opioid exposure unknown – need to calculate baseline

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Inadequate analgesia from wrong conversion:** Pain score unchanged or worse after switch  
  - *Differentiator:* Review actual dose given vs calculated; check for missed breakthrough doses  
- **Opioid toxicity from over-conversion:** Sedation, confusion, reduced respiratory rate  
  - *Splitter:* Sedation score ≥2 (drowsy but rousable) + RR <12 → likely toxicity, not undertreatment  
- **Neuropathic pain not responding to opioids:** Pain is burning, shooting, or allodynic  
  - *Differentiator:* Add gabapentinoid or amitriptyline, not escalate opioid  
- **Opioid-induced hyperalgesia:** Paradoxical worsening of pain on opioid escalation  
  - *Differentiator:* Pain worsens with increasing dose, no new pathology; reduce opioid or rotate

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** Pain score (0-10), sedation score (1-4), respiratory rate, SpO₂  
- **Bloods:** U&Es and creatinine (estimate eGFR for morphine, codeine); LFTs if using methadone or buprenorphine  
- **Imaging:** Not routine unless suspect new pathology (e.g. spinal cord compression)  
- **Special tests:** Opioid plasma levels **not** routinely available or useful  
- **Never-miss test:** **eGFR** before converting to morphine or codeine – active metabolites accumulate (morphine-6-glucuronide, codeine → morphine) → risk of toxicity in renal impairment

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ward start):**  
  - Confirm eGFR, baseline pain score, sedation level  
  - Calculate total daily dose of current opioid (all routes – regular + breakthrough)  
  - Use an **equianalgesic conversion table** (see Drugs & Doses)  
  - **Reduce the calculated dose by 25-50%** for complete cross-tolerance (especially when switching from high doses or between classes)  
  - Prescribe **immediate-release (IR) breakthrough** every 1-2 hours PRN at 10-20% of the regular 24-hour dose (e.g. for morphine oral 60mg/24h → PRN 5-10mg PO 1-2 hourly)  
  - Document new regular opioid + PRN in drug chart, sign and time  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Reassess pain at 4-6 hours post-switch; if pain >5, give breakthrough and consider increasing regular dose by 25-50% after 24h (do not escalate faster)  
  - Monitor sedation score, RR, SpO₂ every 4 hours for 24 hours after rotation  
  - If using **fentanyl patch**: apply to clean dry non-hairy skin; onset 12-24h; do not use for acute pain or unstable doses – allow 12h overlap with oral opioid, then stop previous opioid  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Once stable (pain controlled, stable dose for 48h), switch to long-acting formulation (modified-release)  
  - Arrange GP/pain clinic follow-up for continued monitoring  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: nil special unless NBM – then IV maintenance (e.g. 1L 0.9% NaCl + 1L 5% glucose/24h)  
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40mg SC OD) unless contraindicated  
  - Diet: normal, but encourage high-fibre + fluids to prevent constipation  
  - Observations: NEWS2, plus sedation score and pain score 4-hourly

### Drugs & Doses
- **Equianalgesic conversions** (oral morphine equivalent – OME):  
  - Morphine PO : IV = 3 : 1 (e.g. 10mg PO ≈ 3.3mg IV)  
  - Codeine 60mg ≈ Morphine 6mg PO  
  - Oxycodone PO : Morphine PO = 1 : 1.5 (i.e. 10mg oxycodone ≈ 15mg morphine)  
  - Tramadol 100mg PO ≈ Morphine 10mg PO (weak evidence – use cautiously)  
  - Fentanyl patch conversion: **[check local policy]** – typically 12.5mcg/h patch ≈ oral morphine 30mg/24h (some sources say 25mcg/h ≈ 60mg/24h; **never assume without verifying**)  
  - Buprenorphine patch conversion: **[check local policy]** – low strength (5, 10, 20mcg/h) for moderate pain; do **not** use for high opioid requirement  
  - **Breakthrough dose:** IR morphine/oxycodone 10-20% of 24-hour OME per dose  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - IV morphine (for NBM/acute) – usual starting dose 2.5-5mg IV, titrate  
  - Oxycodone PO or IV (no active metabolite, better in renal impairment) – IV dose 1/2 of PO dose  
  - Fentanyl IV (short-acting, for procedures or renal failure)  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - Avoid codeine/tramadol in CYP2D6 poor metabolisers (no effect) or ultra-rapid metabolisers (toxicity) – check local guidance  
  - Avoid morphine in acute renal impairment (eGFR <30) – use fentanyl, oxycodone or alfentanil  
  - Avoid buprenorphine in severe respiratory compromise or opioid dependence (precipitates withdrawal)  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - **Renal impairment (eGFR <30):** avoid morphine, codeine, dihydrocodeine; reduce oxycodone by 50% or use fentanyl/alfentanil  
  - **Hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh B/C):** reduce morphine/oxycodone/fentanyl dose by 50%; avoid tramadol  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Not reducing dose when rotating to a different opioid** – assuming 1:1 conversion without accounting for incomplete cross-tolerance → respiratory arrest. **Always reduce by 25-50%.**  
  - **Fentanyl patch – cutting it or applying to compromised skin** – rapid release → overdose. **Never cut.**  
  - **Using a fentanyl patch for breakthrough pain** – onset is too slow.

### Complications
- **Acute:** respiratory depression, sedation, falls (especially in elderly), severe constipation (opioid-induced bowel dysfunction)  
- **Chronic:** tolerance, dependence, opioid-induced hyperalgesia, hypogonadism, immunosuppression (long-term)

### Prognosis
- With careful conversion and titration, 70-80% of patients will achieve adequate analgesia; failure often requires specialist pain team input  
- Opioid rotation success rate ~60%; when it fails, consider multimodal analgesia and referral

### Pearls (positive actions)
- Always **calculate the total daily dose** (regular + breakthrough) before any switch – include all PRN doses given in last 24h  
- Document the **conversion method** in the notes (e.g. “morphine 60mg/24h PO → oxycodone 40mg/24h PO, with 50% reduction for cross-tolerance”)  
- Provide a written **pain plan** on discharge: regular dose + PRN regimen, and who to contact if not working

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- Do NOT use **codeine or tramadol** as breakthrough for a patient on high-dose morphine – they are weak and may not work  
- Do NOT switch directly from oral morphine to a fentanyl patch without calculating dose – **always use an equianalgesic table and check local policy**  
- Do NOT forget to **stop the previous opioid** when initiating transdermal patch – list both as regular once only for first 24h, then discontinue old opioid

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** pain not controlled after 2-3 breakthrough doses, increased drowsiness or confusion, or new difficulty breathing  
- **Follow-up:** GP within 48h for dose review; pain clinic referral arranged if high-risk or rotation needed

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / pain nurse:** pain score remains ≥7 despite two breakthrough doses within 4 hours, or if rotation calculation is complex (e.g. methadone, multiple opioids)  
- **Call crash team (2222):** respiratory rate <8/min, GCS <9, SpO₂ <90% on air, or severe opioid-induced neurotoxicity (seizures, myoclonus)
